title: FluidMCP Frontend Onboarding — Test Questions
description: Assessment questions covering frontend setup, React architecture, tool execution, environment variables, and server lifecycle. Created to validate onboarding comprehension for new developers joining the FluidMCP project.

questions.question: At which URL path is the FluidMCP frontend UI accessible?
questions.explanation: The frontend is served at /ui. The root / is the FastAPI info endpoint and /docs is the Swagger UI for the backend API.

questions.question: Which build tool is used for the FluidMCP frontend?
questions.explanation: Vite is used as the build tool and dev server. It is configured in vite.config.ts and includes a proxy setup for API calls during development.

questions.question: What React version does the FluidMCP frontend use?
questions.explanation: The frontend uses React 19.2.0 as specified in the tech stack.

questions.question: Which library is used for form state management in the FluidMCP frontend?
questions.explanation: React Hook Form (version 7.71.1) is used for form state management, paired with Zod for schema validation.

questions.question: What global state management solution does the FluidMCP frontend use?
options: Custom hooks only — no global state library
questions.answer: Custom hooks only — no global state library
questions.explanation: FluidMCP uses custom hooks per-feature for state management. There is no Redux, Zustand, or similar library.

questions.question: Where does production frontend code live after a build?
questions.explanation: npm run build outputs the production bundle to fluidmcp/frontend/dist/. This folder is gitignored and served by the Python backend.

questions.question: Which environment variable does the frontend read to determine the backend base URL?
questions.explanation: The frontend reads VITE_API_BASE_URL. If not set, it defaults to window.location.origin — the same origin it is served from.

questions.question: What is the correct order of steps to set up the frontend for local development?
options: npm run dev → fmcp serve → open browser
npm install → npm run build → fmcp serve → open browser
pip install -r requirements.txt → npm install → npm run dev
fmcp serve → npm install → npm run build
questions.answer: npm install → npm run build → fmcp serve → open browser
questions.explanation: You must install dependencies, build the frontend to dist/, then start the backend (which serves the built frontend). npm run dev can be used for hot-reload during active development.

questions.question: How does the Python backend ensure that client-side routes (e.g. /ui/servers) work when accessed directly?
options: The backend has a FastAPI route for every possible frontend path
Starlette StaticFiles with html=True serves index.html for all /ui/* paths, letting React Router handle routing
The frontend uses hash-based routing so paths never reach the backend
questions.answer: Starlette StaticFiles with html=True serves index.html for all /ui/* paths, letting React Router handle routing
questions.explanation: fluidmcp/cli/api/frontend.py mounts the dist/ folder under /ui using Starlette StaticFiles(html=True) and a catch-all that returns index.html for any /ui/* path. React Router then renders the correct component client-side.

questions.question: What is the minimum required Node.js version for local development?
questions.explanation: The prerequisites state Node.js 18+ and npm are required for local development.

questions.question: List the 5 steps required to add a new page to the FluidMCP frontend.
questions.answer: 1. Create the page component in src/pages/MyPage.tsx. 2. Add the route in src/App.tsx. 3. Add a nav link in src/components/Navbar.tsx. 4. Create a hook in src/hooks/useMyFeature.ts if the page needs API data. 5. Rebuild with npm run build and restart the backend.
questions.explanation: This follows the quick reference guide in frontend-setup.md.

questions.question: The FMCP_BEARER_TOKEN environment variable must be set manually on Railway. Why?
questions.answer: Railway containers are ephemeral. If FMCP_BEARER_TOKEN is not set, a new token is auto-generated on every container restart, which breaks authentication for any client holding the old token.
questions.explanation: This is a critical deployment detail explained in the frontend-setup.md Railway section.

questions.question: How many pages (routes) does the FluidMCP frontend define in App.tsx?
questions.explanation: There are 9 pages: Dashboard, Status, ServerDetails, ToolRunner, ManageServers, LLMModels, LLMModelDetails, LLMPlayground, and Documentation.

questions.question: Which page is the landing page of the FluidMCP frontend?
questions.explanation: Dashboard is the landing page, served at / and /servers. It shows all configured MCP servers as cards.

questions.question: What is the route path for the ToolRunner page?
questions.explanation: ToolRunner is at /servers/:serverId/tools/:toolName — it requires both the server ID and the tool name as path parameters.

questions.question: Which page is intentionally separate from Dashboard because its purpose is configuration rather than monitoring?
questions.explanation: ManageServers is the CRUD control panel at /servers/manage. It is intentionally separate — Dashboard is for monitoring, ManageServers is for configuration.

questions.question: Why can you NOT deep-link directly to a route like /servers from outside the app?
options: The backend only serves the SPA from /ui — routes like /servers are not backend routes
React Router does not support direct URL navigation
The routes are hash-based and cannot be linked
questions.answer: The backend only serves the SPA from /ui — routes like /servers are not backend routes
questions.explanation: The Python backend only mounts the frontend under /ui. Paths like /servers are client-side routes handled by React Router, so you must start from /ui and navigate within the SPA.

questions.question: What is the only place in the codebase where apiClient methods are called?
questions.explanation: All hooks live in src/hooks/ and are the only place that calls apiClient methods. Pages and components call hooks, not apiClient directly.

questions.question: Which hook does the ManageServers page use?
questions.explanation: ManageServers uses useServerManagement, which provides createServer, updateServer, deleteServer operations and fetches servers including disabled ones.

questions.question: What happens when a user tries to edit a server that is currently running in ManageServers?
options: The server is automatically stopped before the edit form opens
The edit is blocked with an alert — the user must stop the server first
The form opens but changes are queued until the server is stopped
The edit is allowed for non-critical fields only
questions.answer: The edit is blocked with an alert — the user must stop the server first
questions.explanation: The handleEdit guard in ManageServers checks server.status?.state === 'running' and shows an alert if true. The user must stop the server before editing.

questions.question: Which hook does LLMModels use that Dashboard does NOT, and what is its purpose?
options: useDebounce — delays search input updates to avoid excessive filtering on each keystroke
useMemo — memoizes the list of models
useRef — tracks whether the component is mounted
questions.answer: useDebounce — delays search input updates to avoid excessive filtering on each keystroke
questions.explanation: LLMModels uses useDebounce(searchQuery, 300) which waits 300ms after the user stops typing before applying the filter. Dashboard does inline filtering without debounce.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the AbortController + isMountedRef pattern used in hooks?
options: To cancel fetch requests that take longer than 30 seconds
To prevent state updates on an unmounted component, avoiding memory leaks
To batch multiple API calls into a single request
questions.answer: To prevent state updates on an unmounted component, avoiding memory leaks
questions.explanation: If a component unmounts while a fetch is in flight, calling setState on it would cause a React warning and potential memory leak. AbortController cancels the request and isMountedRef guards against any state updates after unmount.

questions.question: Why is useCallback used on fetch functions inside hooks?
options: To make the fetch function run faster
To memoize the function reference so useEffect does not re-run infinitely when the function is a dependency
To ensure the fetch runs on the main thread
To share the same function instance across multiple components
questions.answer: To memoize the function reference so useEffect does not re-run infinitely when the function is a dependency
questions.explanation: Without useCallback, a new function reference is created on every render. If that function is in the useEffect dependency array, it triggers the effect on every render, causing an infinite loop.

questions.question: After calling createServer(), updateServer(), or deleteServer() in useServerManagement, what always happens next?
options: The UI updates optimistically without a server round-trip
fetchServers() is called again to re-sync the UI with the server state
The component is forced to unmount and remount
A WebSocket event triggers a global state refresh
questions.answer: fetchServers() is called again to re-sync the UI with the server state
questions.explanation: Every mutation calls fetchData() at the end to keep the UI in sync with the server. This is a common pattern across all hooks in FluidMCP.

questions.question: The Documentation page makes no API calls and uses no custom hooks. Why is this architecturally correct?
options: Documentation pages should always be server-rendered
The content is static and hardcoded in the component — no server data is needed
It is a temporary implementation that will be refactored later
The hook pattern does not support static content
questions.answer: The content is static and hardcoded in the component — no server data is needed
questions.explanation: Documentation uses local useState only for UI interactions (active section, sidebar open state, scroll position). Creating a hook just to wrap static content would add unnecessary complexity.

questions.question: How many custom hooks are defined in src/hooks/ (excluding use-toast.ts and use-mobile.tsx)?
questions.explanation: The hooks overview table lists 11 custom hooks: useServers, useServerManagement, useServerDetails, useServerEnv, useServerPolling, useActiveServerFiltering, useServerFiltering, useLLMModels, useToolRunner, usePolling, useDebounce.

questions.question: Describe the full data flow from user action to UI update in the FluidMCP hook pattern.
questions.answer: Page/Component calls a custom hook. The hook calls an apiClient method (e.g. apiClient.listServers()). apiClient calls fetch() to the FastAPI backend. The backend returns a typed response. The hook updates its state. The state change triggers a re-render in the component.
questions.explanation: This is the hook → api → component pattern described in frontend-setup.md.

questions.question: How is the ApiClient used across the codebase?
options: A new instance is created in each hook
It is imported as a class and instantiated per page
It is a singleton — one instance exported from services/api.ts and imported everywhere
It is injected via React Context
questions.answer: It is a singleton — one instance exported from services/api.ts and imported everywhere
questions.explanation: services/api.ts ends with 'export default new ApiClient()'. This singleton is imported by all hooks. Only one instance exists for the entire app.

questions.question: What class is thrown when an HTTP response has a non-2xx status code?
questions.explanation: ApiHttpError extends Error and carries both the message and the HTTP status code. Hooks catch it to surface meaningful error messages in the UI.

questions.question: What is the default request timeout for most ApiClient methods?
questions.explanation: The private request() method sets a 30-second timeout using AbortController. GitHub operations use a longer 120-second timeout.

questions.question: Which file is the ONLY file that calls fetch() in the entire frontend?
questions.explanation: All HTTP calls go through the ApiClient singleton in services/api.ts. No hook or component should call fetch() directly — they must go through apiClient methods.

questions.question: What happens to an MCP server's configuration when it is added via the API?
options: It is stored in a local JSON config file
It is stored in environment variables
questions.explanation: When a server is added, its config is validated and stored in MongoDB for persistence across restarts.

questions.question: What are the two delete options available in ManageServers?
questions.explanation: Soft-delete (disable) keeps the server in the database with enabled=false and allows recovery. Hard delete permanently removes it from MongoDB.

questions.question: What communication protocol does the backend use to perform the MCP handshake when starting a server?
questions.explanation: When starting an MCP server, the backend spawns a subprocess and communicates via JSON-RPC messages over the process's stdin/stdout pipes. This is the MCP protocol.

questions.question: What must a developer do before editing a running MCP server's configuration?
options: Create a copy of the server first
Put the server in maintenance mode
questions.explanation: Editing a running server is blocked in the UI. The user must stop the server first, then edit its configuration. This prevents inconsistent state.

questions.question: What does the restart flow consist of?
options: A single API call that atomically restarts the process
Kill the process and wait for auto-recovery
questions.explanation: Restart is a sequential Stop → Start. There is no atomic restart — it is two separate operations.

questions.question: After starting an MCP server, what additional data does the backend cache?
options: The tool list returned by the MCP handshake
questions.answer: The tool list returned by the MCP handshake
questions.explanation: After the MCP handshake during startup, the backend caches the tool list. This avoids re-running the handshake every time a client requests available tools.

questions.question: Where does tool execution history get stored in the FluidMCP frontend?
options: In MongoDB via the backend API
questions.explanation: Tool execution history is persisted in localStorage via services/toolHistory.ts. The history is per-tool and capped at 50 entries.

questions.question: What is the entry path to execute a tool in the FluidMCP UI?
questions.explanation: The tool execution entry path is Dashboard → ServerDetails → ToolRunner. The user navigates to a server's details page first, then selects a tool to run.

questions.question: How does the ToolRunner generate its input form?
options: The form is hardcoded for each known tool
The form is auto-generated from the tool's JSON Schema definition
The user types raw JSON input
The form is fetched from the backend as HTML
questions.answer: The form is auto-generated from the tool's JSON Schema definition
questions.explanation: JsonSchemaForm.tsx auto-generates forms from tool schemas. Each field type (string, number, boolean, array, object) has a corresponding renderer component.

questions.question: What are the four result display formats that ToolResult.tsx can auto-detect?
questions.explanation: ToolResult.tsx auto-detects the result format and delegates to JsonResultView, TableResultView, TextResultView, or McpContentView accordingly.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of tool execution history entries stored per tool in localStorage?
questions.explanation: Tool history is capped at 50 entries per tool, as implemented in services/toolHistory.ts.

questions.question: Where in the ServerDetails page are environment variables managed?
questions.explanation: ServerDetails has three tabs: Tools, Logs, and Env. Environment variables are managed in the Env tab.

questions.question: What happens to env var values when they are read back from the backend?
options: They are returned in plain text
They are masked — sensitive values are not exposed
questions.answer: They are masked — sensitive values are not exposed
questions.explanation: The backend masks env var values on read. This prevents API keys and secrets from being exposed in the UI or API responses.

questions.question: When do environment variable changes take effect for a running MCP server?
options: Immediately — the running process picks them up automatically
After the browser page is refreshed
Only after the server is restarted
After 60 seconds via a background sync
questions.answer: Only after the server is restarted
questions.explanation: Env var edits apply only to the running instance via PUT /api/servers/:id/instance/env. For the changes to persist and take full effect across restarts, the server must be restarted.

questions.question: Which hook handles env variable CRUD for a server, and what API endpoints does it use?
options: useServerEnv — GET /api/servers/:id/instance/env for reads and PUT /api/servers/:id/instance/env for writes
questions.answer: useServerEnv — GET /api/servers/:id/instance/env for reads and PUT /api/servers/:id/instance/env for writes
questions.explanation: useServerEnv is dedicated to env var CRUD. It reads from GET /api/servers/:id/instance/env and writes to PUT /api/servers/:id/instance/env.

questions.question: Where are TypeScript type definitions for servers, tools, and env vars located?
questions.explanation: src/types/server.ts contains Server, Tool, and EnvVar TypeScript types. LLM-related types are in src/types/llm.ts.

questions.question: Which file defines ALL client-side routes for the application?
questions.explanation: src/App.tsx is the single source of truth for all route definitions. It sets up React Router DOM with all 9 page routes.

questions.question: What utility function is exported from src/lib/utils.ts?
questions.explanation: src/lib/utils.ts exports cn(), a utility for conditional and merged Tailwind CSS class names. It is used throughout components.

questions.question: Which folder contains the auto-generated form components for rendering tool input fields?
questions.explanation: src/components/form/ contains JsonSchemaForm.tsx and field renderers (StringInput, NumberInput, BooleanInput, ArrayInput, ObjectInput, SelectInput) that auto-generate forms from tool JSON schemas.

questions.question: What is the purpose of src/components/ui/ versus src/components/ (top level)?
options: ui/ is for server-side rendered components; top level is for client-side
ui/ contains shadcn/ui primitives (Button, Dialog, etc.); top level contains feature-specific composed components
ui/ is deprecated; top level is the current standard
ui/ is for mobile components; top level is for desktop
questions.answer: ui/ contains shadcn/ui primitives (Button, Dialog, etc.); top level contains feature-specific composed components
questions.explanation: src/components/ui/ holds the base Radix UI / shadcn/ui primitives. The top-level src/components/ holds feature-specific components like ServerCard, ServerForm, Navbar, etc. that compose those primitives.

topics_covered: Server lifecycle (add, start, stop, restart, delete)
Tool execution flow and JSON Schema forms
